mnemonics.title: Try-Catch-Throw Order
mnemonics.content: Think of it as 'Try to do something, Throw if it fails, Catch to handle the failure' - the order mirrors the logical flow of attempting an operation and handling problems.

mnemonics.title: Stack Unwinding Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Like unwinding a coiled rope - as exceptions go up, local objects get 'unwound' (destroyed) from the stack in reverse order of creation.

mnemonics.title: Catch Order Matters
mnemonics.content: Most specific (derived) exceptions first, then general (base) exceptions - like catching specific fish before using a net that catches everything.

acronyms.usage: C++ technique where resources are tied to object lifetime, crucial for exception-safe resource management

acronyms.expansion: No Exception - indicates function promises not to throw
acronyms.usage: Used to mark functions that will not throw, enabling compiler optimizations
Provides standard exception classes used in exception handling

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a stack of plates. When an exception is thrown, plates (local objects) fall off one by one from top to bottom as control moves up the call stack.
Draw an arrow from try block → throw → catch block. If no catch matches, arrow goes to std::terminate() with skull symbol.
Think of catch blocks as different-sized nets. Smaller nets (derived classes) must be placed before larger nets (base classes) to catch specific fish.

analogies.analogy: Like a safety net in circus: try is the performer, throw is when something goes wrong, catch is the safety net catching them.
Like a series of nested boxes. When an error occurs in the innermost box, you close each box (destroy local objects) as you pass it to get to the handler outside.
Like biological classification: std::exception is the 'Mammal', std::runtime_error is 'Carnivore', specific errors are like 'Lion' or 'Tiger'.
